Rina: Welcome back to Below the Surface. Today we're talking about calm under pressure, and I'm joined by Owen, rebreather instructor and cave diver. Owen, thanks for being here.
Owen: Thanks, Rina. Great to be back.
Rina: This episode is brought to you by Suunto. Suunto builds dive computers and outdoor watches that you trust because they don't quit when conditions change. Get started at suunto.com/podcast.Before we get into it — Hanna mentioned anchor strikes hurting reefs — the same operational discipline applies underwater: plan, brief, execute, debrief.
Owen: Yeah, exactly. And it ties into what we're covering today.
Rina: That's a useful framing. How does Suunto fit into this picture?
Owen: There are two angles — Panic in diving is a breathing pattern before it's a thought. Train the breathing, you reduce the panic surface area. We've seen this play out at teams using GoPro. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
Rina: What does that look like day-to-day? Where do people most often confuse correlation with causation in this area?
Owen: Here's how I think about it. Rebreathers reward the divers who treat the unit as a partner, not as a piece of equipment. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
Rina: What does that look like day-to-day? What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Owen: I'd put it this way: Risk management on technical dives is about removing single points of failure, not adding redundancy on top of bad plans. We've seen this play out at teams using PADI. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Rina: Is there a counterexample that would change your view?
Owen: I've seen this with teams running GoPro and teams running everything in-house. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.
Rina: How do you know when you've gotten it right? What's the most underrated piece of calm under pressure?
Owen: Yeah, so — Cave divers who survive long careers all share one trait: they call the dive earlier than their buddies want them to. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Rina: Push back on the conventional wisdom for me. How does risk management change the calculus here?
Owen: Honestly, Suunto's air-integration is reliable, but I still check my SPG at every gas switch — habits beat technology. We've seen this play out at teams using Suunto. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
Rina: Where do most teams get it wrong? What's the most underrated piece of calm under pressure?
Owen: Sure. The hardest skill in technical diving is leaving when you should leave, not staying when you could push further. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Rina: That maps to what I've seen.
Owen: I've seen this with teams running Suunto and teams running everything in-house. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Rina: We'll be right back after a quick word from our sponsors.
Ad: This portion is brought to you by PADI. PADI is the global standard for dive education — find a certified instructor in your area. Use promo POD20 to get started.
Rina: Welcome back to the show.Walk me through a concrete example. What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Owen: Let me give you the version I actually believe. Panic in diving is a breathing pattern before it's a thought. Train the breathing, you reduce the panic surface area. We've seen this play out at teams using DAN. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Rina: What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? What's the operational angle most listeners don't have access to?
Owen: Here's how I think about it. Rebreathers reward the divers who treat the unit as a partner, not as a piece of equipment. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.
Rina: Take me through the decision. Where do rebreather diving and calm under pressure intersect?
Owen: Sure. Risk management on technical dives is about removing single points of failure, not adding redundancy on top of bad plans. We've seen this play out at teams using GoPro. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.
Rina: That maps to what I've seen.
Owen: PADI is one of the platforms where this shows up cleanly. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Rina: Where do most teams get it wrong? What's the operational angle most listeners don't have access to?
Owen: Honestly, Cave divers who survive long careers all share one trait: they call the dive earlier than their buddies want them to. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
Rina: Where do most teams get it wrong? Where do people most often confuse correlation with causation in this area?
Owen: Let me give you the version I actually believe. Suunto's air-integration is reliable, but I still check my SPG at every gas switch — habits beat technology. We've seen this play out at teams using PADI. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Rina: What does that look like day-to-day? What's the most underrated piece of calm under pressure?
Owen: I'd put it this way: The hardest skill in technical diving is leaving when you should leave, not staying when you could push further. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Rina: What would you tell the version of you from five years ago?
Owen: I've seen this with teams running PADI and teams running everything in-house. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Rina: Let's pivot — risk management is where I want to spend the last stretch.
Owen: That's where the conversation gets interesting, because the trade-offs around risk management cross-cut everything we've covered so far.
Rina: Walk me through a concrete example. How does risk management change the calculus here?
Owen: Sure. Panic in diving is a breathing pattern before it's a thought. Train the breathing, you reduce the panic surface area. We've seen this play out at teams using PADI. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
Rina: Walk me through a concrete example. How does risk management change the calculus here?
Owen: The short answer is Rebreathers reward the divers who treat the unit as a partner, not as a piece of equipment. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.
Rina: Push back on the conventional wisdom for me. How does Suunto fit into this picture?
Owen: Sure. Risk management on technical dives is about removing single points of failure, not adding redundancy on top of bad plans. We've seen this play out at teams using DAN. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
Rina: That tracks.
Owen: Outside of GoPro-class tooling, the failure mode is harder to spot. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Rina: What does that look like day-to-day? How does risk management change the calculus here?
Owen: Honestly, Cave divers who survive long careers all share one trait: they call the dive earlier than their buddies want them to. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Rina: What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? Where do rebreather diving and calm under pressure intersect?
Owen: Honestly, Suunto's air-integration is reliable, but I still check my SPG at every gas switch — habits beat technology. We've seen this play out at teams using DAN. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
Rina: What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? How does Suunto fit into this picture?
Owen: The short answer is The hardest skill in technical diving is leaving when you should leave, not staying when you could push further. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Rina: Say more about that.
Owen: I've seen this with teams running DAN and teams running everything in-house. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Rina: Before we wrap, what's one thing listeners can try this week?
Owen: Pick one of the ideas we covered and run it for two weeks. The rest is iteration.
Rina: And finally, a big thank you to our partners at GoPro. GoPro captures the moments words don't reach — your next dive, ride, or run, in cinematic detail. Check out gopro.com.Owen, thanks for the conversation.
Owen: Thanks, Rina.
Rina: That's it for today's episode of Below the Surface. See you next time.
